title: Unpacking Octopus Intelligence: Distributed Minds in Problem-Solving
contentMarkdown:
- Octopus vulgaris exhibits cognitive complexities rare among invertebrates.
- This study investigated their spatial problem-solving, focusing on tactile vs. visual sensory input.
- We used transparent and opaque puzzle boxes to observe how they navigate complex latches.
- Results show octopuses prioritize haptic feedback from their arms over visual confirmation.
- This suggests a "distributed" cognitive model where intelligence is decentralized.

title: Tactile-First Strategy: Experimental Design and Surprising Efficiency
contentMarkdown:
- Six sub-adult Octopus vulgaris were subjects in a "Two-Stage Latch Box" experiment.
- Phase A (Visual): Clear boxes allowed visual cues of the reward and latch mechanism.
- Phase B (Tactile): Opaque boxes forced reliance on chemical and tactile sensors in their suckers.
- We measured Time to Entry (TTE) and Number of Errors (NE).
- Surprisingly, octopuses showed no significant increase in TTE in opaque boxes, and some even solved puzzles faster, suggesting visual input can be a "distractor."

title: Decentralized Cognition: Implications and Future Research
contentMarkdown:
- Our findings strongly support the hypothesis of Decentralized Cognition, where two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are in its arms.
- In opaque boxes, the peripheral nervous system in the arms efficiently "felt" the mechanical logic of the latch.
- Each sucker contains tens of thousands of chemoreceptors and mechanoreceptors, making tactile processing highly effective.
- This raises significant questions about consciousness: Can an arm possess its own localized form of intent?
- Future research should explore if arms can "communicate" mechanical solutions or if learning is localized to the specific limb used.
